danger_signals.visual_form: Alien sprites positioned in level pathways
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring avoidance or elimination
Destructible obstacles blocking progression; level-gating mechanic
Impassable obstacles requiring keycards or cloaking device to bypass
Progression gates requiring key items to open

danger_signals.visual_form: Player sprite taking damage from contact with enemies
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate physical threat to player survival

safety_signals.meaning: Resource for restoring player vitality after damage

safety_signals.visual_form: Distinct item sprites (flamethrower, laser, rocket launcher)
safety_signals.meaning: Power-up resources for eliminating threats more effectively
Temporary invincibility and force field bypass capability
Increased weapon effectiveness for threat mitigation

safety_signals.visual_form: Goal point at end of platforming sequence
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression to next challenge; checkpoint

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: None documented; player has singular visual identity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player sprite navigating anthropomorphic levels; protagonist presence indicates human/allied territory

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Alien enemy sprites as hostile opposition throughout levels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Alien technology (radar dishes, force fields, mothership); presence in planetary and underground environments indicates alien occupation/invasion
Urban environment (Neo LA); infrastructure requiring keys and keycards suggests human-built security systems; captured protagonist on alien flagship indicates human territory being invaded

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Alien invasion in progress (1998 narrative context)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rigelatins occupying Earth; presence of alien enemies throughout levels; radar dishes as surveillance infrastructure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Duke must fight through alien-controlled territories, destroy energy infrastructure (reactor, mothership), suggesting active combat occupation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Initial level starts with Duke in cell environment; must escape through infrastructure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player begins in confined space; progression represents escape from captivity and liberation of self through level-to-level advancement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level environments transition from surface structures to subterranean areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player fights across multiple terrain types, suggesting full-scale invasion requiring defense of both surface and deep installations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Destruction of radar dishes, energy reactor, mothership as episode progressions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Each episode escalates threat level; final episode culminates in mothership destruction and jet hijacking escape

environmental_storytelling.example: Four episodes of eight levels each; progression from surface (Neo LA) → underground → alien installations → mothership indicates deepening into enemy territory
Keycards, force fields, elevators, teleporters, fans, and hand-holds indicate militarized/industrial human infrastructure being infiltrated and repurposed by aliens
Radar dishes as destructible level-gating elements; their presence indicates surveillance infrastructure maintaining alien control
Health items and weapons scattered strategically; their availability/scarcity across levels indicates difficulty progression and environmental threat density

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Jumps, climbs, uses platforms, collects items, fires weapons; capable of operating level infrastructure (elevators, teleporters)
Positioned as obstacles in level pathways; attack Duke on contact; require weapon engagement or avoidance to bypass
Remain stationary; destructible through weapon fire; must be eliminated as level progression requirement
Block passage until disabled by cloaking device or keycard acquisition; visual indication of restricted zones
Culminating encounter requiring sustained combat; presence indicates climactic narrative confrontation

notes: Duke Nukem II employs straightforward visual communication typical of 1993 DOS platformers. The world-state is communicated primarily through progression structure (episodic escalation), enemy placement (occupation indicator), and environmental props (infrastructure and security systems). Faction identity is implicit rather than explicit—human vs. alien is signaled through technology aesthetics and narrative context rather than explicit visual sigils. The Rigelatins' world-state manifests through their physical presence in levels and control of technology. No documented dynamic environmental responses to player actions beyond item destruction and enemy elimination.
